title: The Top 10 Films of the Year | Discussion (Ep. 105)
format: Sean Fennessey, Chris Ryan, and Adam Nayman each count down their personal top ten films of 2018, comparing notes whenever a movie lands on more than one list.

segments.heading: Chris Ryan's top 10
segments.note: best to worst, as revealed on air
segments.subject: Chris Ryan's 2018

films.title: Roma
films.note: No. 1 - the Brooklyn Bridge comparison: a technical marvel he also felt something in, watched in a packed, crying theater.

films.title: Wildlife
films.note: No. 2 - Paul Dano's directing debut. His 'Lady Bird' of the year - needed a moment alone after it ended.

films.title: Burning
films.note: No. 3 - tied with Nayman as the highest-ranked film either of them has. A movie that made him fall in love with movies in the first place.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: No. 4 - made him laugh harder than anything this year outside Death of Stalin; loves the Weisz/Stone/Colman troika.

films.title: Sicario: Day of the Soldado
films.note: No. 5 - 'a doom metal song of a Hollywood blockbuster.' Admires its refusal to give the audience a moral handhold.

films.title: Thunder Road
films.note: No. 6 - Jim Cummings' indie built out of his own Sundance short. The eulogy that opens it floored him.

films.title: Widows
films.note: No. 7 - Steve McQueen's grimy answer to Ocean's Eleven; a heist movie about what people owe each other.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: No. 9 - the best Marvel movie, thinks the Killmonger/T'Challa dynamic is the genre's best pairing since The Dark Knight.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: No. 10 - the first hour as an achievement in old-school star-making, even if it can't sustain that through the whole runtime.

segments.heading: Adam Nayman's top 10
segments.note: best to worst, as revealed on air
segments.subject: Adam Nayman's 2018

films.title: Zama
films.note: No. 1 - Lucrezia Martel's Argentine colonial satire. Seen it three times; calls her one of the most singular filmmakers working.

films.title: Let the Sun Shine In
films.note: No. 2 - Claire Denis, Juliette Binoche. Reads as gentle but is, per Denis herself, one of her harshest films.

films.title: Burning
films.note: No. 3 - loved the screenplay above all: class, masculinity, and hunger all embedded in the writing itself.

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: No. 4 - Barry Jenkins' Baldwin adaptation. Singles out Brian Tyree Henry's jail monologue as maybe the scene of the year.

films.title: Support the Girls
films.note: No. 5 - Andrew Bujalski. Regina Hall's performance is his female lead performance of the year.

films.title: Happy as Lazzaro
films.note: No. 6 - Alice Rohrwacher, on Netflix. A gentle-seeming political allegory that turns into something else at the midpoint.

films.title: The Ballad of Buster Scruggs
films.note: No. 7 - the Coens' anthology film. A movie about mortality told across six wildly different registers.

films.title: Bodied
films.note: No. 8 - Joseph Kahn's battle-rap movie, produced by Eminem. 'Not the best movie of 2018, but the most 2018 of any movie.'

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: No. 9 - Paul Schrader, Ethan Hawke. The environmental-anxiety plot handled 'with a lot of grace.'

films.title: Infinite Football
films.note: No. 10 - a Corneliu Porumboiu documentary about a man trying to rewrite soccer's rules. The funniest thing he saw all year.

segments.heading: Sean Fennessey's top 10
segments.note: the picks he named on air, best to worst
segments.subject: Sean Fennessey's 2018

films.title: Minding the Gap
films.note: No. 1 - Bing Liu's skateboarding documentary. The most moving thing he saw all year; ties it back to a Michael Moore film from 1998 shot in the same town.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: Also on his list - keeps trying to get his wife to rewatch it with him.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Also on his list, distinct from Chris's ranking of it - drawn to how much intentional thematic weight Ryan Coogler smuggled into a studio tentpole.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Fallout
films.note: The best action movie in years for him - saw it three times in theaters.

films.title: Hereditary
films.note: Ari Aster's follow-up he's most anticipating a career from; calls two sequences the year's most visceral filmmaking.

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: An elegant, powerful Baldwin adaptation - not out until December 14, so he's still sitting with it.

films.title: Annihilation
films.note: Alex Garland's loose adaptation of the VanderMeer novel - admires the bravery of it even where it doesn't fully land.

films: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
